author: John Chrysostom
book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles

ccel.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily XXIX on Acts xiii. 16, 17.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul stood up, and beckoning with his hand said, Men of Israel, and ye that fear God, give audience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The God of this people of Israel chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they dwelt as strangers in the land of Egypt, and with an high arm brought He them out of it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold Barnabas giving place to Paul—how should it be otherwise?—to him whom he brought from Tarsus; just as we find John on all occasions giving way to Peter: and yet Barnabas was more looked up to than Paul: true, but they had an eye only to the common advantage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then Paul stood up,” it says;—this was a custom of the Jews—“and beckoned with his hand.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how he prepares the way beforehand for his discourse: having first praised them, and showed his great regard for them in the words, “ye that fear God,” he so begins his discourse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he says not, Ye proselytes, since it was a term of disadvantage.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The God of this people chose our fathers: and the people”—See, he calls God Himself their God peculiarly, Who is the common God of men; and shows how great from the first were His benefits, just as Stephen does.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This they do to teach them, that now also God has acted after the same custom, in sending His own Son; (Luke xx.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13): as (Christ) Himself (does) in the parable of the vineyard—“And the people,” he says, “He exalted when it sojourned in the land of Egypt”—and yet the contrary was the case: true, but they increased in numbers; moreover, the miracles were wrought on their account: “and with an high arm brought He them out of it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of these things (the wonders) which were done in Egypt, the prophets are continually making mention.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, how he passes over the times of their calamities, and nowhere brings forward their faults, but only God’s kindness, leaving those for themselves to think over.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And about the time of forty years suffered He their manners in the wilderness.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the settlement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, He divided their land to them by lot.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the time was long; four hundred and fifty years.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after that He gave unto them judges about the space of four hundred and fifty years, until Samuel the prophet.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here he shows that God varied His dispensations towards them (at divers times).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And afterward they desired a king:” and (still) not a word of their ingratitude, but throughout he speaks of the kindness of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And God gave unto them Saul the son of Cis, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, by the space of forty years.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had removed him, He raised up unto them David to be their king: to whom also He gave testimony, and said, I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after Mine own heart, which shall fulfil all My will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of this man’s seed hath God according to His promise raised unto Israel a Saviour, Jesus.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was no small thing that Christ should be from David.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then John bears witness to this: “When John had first preached before His coming the baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as John fulfilled his course, he said, Whom think ye that I am?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am not He.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, behold, there cometh one after me, whose shoes of His feet I am not worthy to loose.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And John too not merely bears witness (to the fact), but (does it in such sort that) when men were bringing the glory to him, he declines it: for it is one thing (not to affect) an honor which nobody thinks of offering; and another, to reject it when all men are ready to give it, and not only to reject it, but to do so with such humility.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men and brethren, children of the stock of Abraham, and whosoever among you feareth God, to you is the word of this salvation sent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For they that dwell at Jerusalem, and their rulers, because they knew Him not, nor yet the voices of the prophets which are read every sabbath day, they have fulfilled them in condemning Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And though they found no cause of death in Him, yet desired they Pilate that He should be slain.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: On all occasions we find them making a great point of showing this, that the blessing is peculiarly theirs, that they may not flee (from Christ), as thinking they had nothing to do with Him, because they had crucified Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because they knew Him not,” he says: so that the sin was one of ignorance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how he gently makes an apology even on behalf of those (crucifiers).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And not only this: but he adds also, that thus it must needs be.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “By condemning Him, they fulfilled the voices of the prophets.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then again from the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had fulfilled all that was written of Him, they took Him down from the tree, and laid Him in a sepulchre.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But God raised Him from the dead.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And He was seen many days of them which came up with Him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are His witnesses unto the people—”(v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 29–31) that He rose again.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And we declare unto you glad tidings, how that the promise which was made unto the fathers, God hath fulfilled the same unto us their children, in that He hath raised up Jesus again; as it is also written in the second Psalm, Thou art My Son, this day have I begotten Thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And as concerning that He raised Him up from the dead, now no more to return to corruption, He said on this wise, I will give you the sure mercies of David.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore he saith also in another Psalm, Thou shalt not suffer Thine Holy One to see corruption.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For David, after he had served his own generation by the will of God, fell on sleep, and was laid unto his fathers, and saw corruption: but He, Whom God raised again, saw no corruption.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be it known unto you therefore, men and brethren, that through this Man is preached unto you the forgiveness of sins: and by Him all that believe are justified from all things, from which ye could not be justified by the law of Moses.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how Paul here is more vehement in his discourse: we nowhere find Peter saying this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then too he adds the terrifying words: “Beware therefore, lest that come upon you, which is spoken of in the prophets; Behold, ye despisers, and wonder, and perish: for I work a work in your days, a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though a man declare it unto you.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (a) Observe how he twines (the thread of) his discourse (alternately) from things present, from the prophets.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, “from (this man’s) seed according to the promise”—(v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 23): (c) the name of David was dear to them; well then, is it not (a thing to be desired) that a son of his, he says, should be their king?—(b) then he adduces John: then again the prophets, where he says, “By condemning they fulfilled,” and again, “All that was written:” then the Apostles as witnesses of the Resurrection: then David bearing witness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For neither the Old Testament proofs seemed so cogent when taken by themselves as they are in this way, nor yet the latter testimonies apart from the former: wherefore he makes them mutually confirm each other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men and brethren,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since they were possessed by fear, as having slain Him, and conscience made them aliens (the Apostles), discourse not with them as unto Christicides, neither as putting into their hands a good which was not theirs, but one peculiarly their own. (d) “For they that dwell at Jerusalem, and their rulers:” as much as to say, not ye, but they: and again, apologizing even for those, “Because they knew Him not, and the voices of the Prophets which are read every sabbath day, in condemning Him, they fulfilled them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A great charge it is against them that they continually hearing heeded not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But no marvel: for what was said above concerning Egypt and the wilderness, was enough to show their ingratitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how this Apostle also, as one moved by the Spirit Himself, continually preaches the Passion, the Burial. (g) “Having taken Him down from the tree.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, what a great point they make of this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He speaks of the manner of His death.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Moreover they bring Pilate (conspicuously) forward, that (the fact of) the Passion may be proved by the mention of the tribunal (by which he was condemned), but at the same time, for the greater impeachment of those (His crucifiers), seeing they delivered Him up to an alien.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, They made a complaint (against Him), (ἐνέτυχον, al. ἐντυγχάνει) but, “They desired, though having found no cause of death” (in Him), “that He should be slain. (e) Who appeared,” he says, “for many days to them that came up with Him from Galilee to Jerusalem.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Instead of** he says, “Who are His witnesses unto the people,” to wit, “The men which came up with Him from Galilee to Jerusalem.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he produces David and Esaias bearing witness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “The faithful (mercies),” the abiding (mercies), those which never perish. (h) Paul loved them exceedingly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, he does not enlarge on the ingratitude of the fathers, but puts before them what they must fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For Stephen indeed with good reason does this, seeing he was about to be put to death, not teaching them; and showing them, that the Law is even now on the point of being abolished: (ch. vii.) but not so Paul; he does but threaten and put them in fear. (f) And he does not dwell long on these, as taking it for granted that the word is of course believed; nor enlarge upon the greatness of their punishment, and assail that which they affectionately love, by showing the Law about to be cast out: but dwells upon that which is for their good (telling them), that great shall be the blessings for them being obedient, and great the evils being disobedient.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Ye men of Israel,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Promise then, he says, the fathers received; ye, the reality. (j) And observe, he nowhere mentions right deeds of theirs, but (only) benefits on God’s part: “He chose: Exalted: Suffered their manners:” these are no matters of praise to them: “They asked, He gave.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But David he does praise (and him) only, because from him the Christ was to come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I have found David, the son of Jesse, a man after Mine own heart, which shall fulfil all My will.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22.) (i) Observe also; it is with praise (that he says of him), “David after that he had served the will of God:” just as Peter—seeing it was then the beginning of the Gospel—making mention of him, said, “Let it be permitted me to speak freely of the patriarch David.” (ch. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Also, he does not say, Died, but, “was added to his fathers. (k) Of this man’s seed,” etc. “When John,” he says, “had first preached before His entry”—by entry he means the Incarnation—“the baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also John, writing his Gospel, continually has recourse to him: for his name was much thought of in all parts of the world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe, he does not say it “Of this man’s seed,” etc. from himself, but brings John’s testimony.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Men and brethren, children of the stock of Abraham”—he also calls them after their father—“unto you was the word of this salvation sent.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here the expression, “Unto you,” does not mean, Unto (you) Jews, but it gives them a right to sever themselves from those who dared that murder.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what he adds, shows this plainly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For,” he says, “they that dwell at Jerusalem, because they know Him not.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And how, you will say, could they be ignorant, with John to tell them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What marvel, seeing they were so, with the prophets continually crying aloud to them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then follows another charge: “And having found no cause of death in Him:” in which ignorance had nothing to do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For let us put the case, that they did not hold Him to be the Christ: why did they also kill Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And “they desired of Pilate, he says, that He should be slain.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when they had fulfilled all that was written of Him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe what a point he makes of showing that the (whole) thing was a (Divine) Dispensation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, by saying what did they persuade men?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (By telling them) that He was crucified?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, what could be less persuasive than this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That He was buried—by them to whom it was promised that He should be salvation? that He who was buried forgives sins, yea, more than the Law (has power to do)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (observe), he does not say, From which ye would not but, “from which ye could not be justified by the Law of Moses.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Every one,” he says: be who he may.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For those (ordinances) are of no use, unless there be some benefit (accruing therefrom.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why he brings in forgiveness later: and shows it to be greater, when, the thing being (otherwise) impossible, yet this is effected.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who are His witnesses,” he says, “unto the people”—the people that slew Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who would never have been so, were they not strengthened by a Divine Power: for they would never have borne such witness to blood-thirsty men, to the very persons that killed Him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But, “He hath raised up Jesus again: This day,” he says, “I have begotten thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Aye, upon this the rest follows of course.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why did he not allege some text by which they would be persuaded that forgiveness of sins is by Him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Because the great point with them was to show, in the first place, that He was risen: this being acknowledged, the other was unquestionable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Through this man,” nay more, by Him, “is remission of sins.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, he wished to bring them to a longing desire of this great thing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then, His death was not dereliction, but fulfilling of Prophecy.—For the rest, he puts them in mind of historical facts, wherein they through ignorance suffered evils without number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this he hints in the conclusion, saying, “Look, ye despisers, and behold.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how, this being harsh, he cuts it short.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let not that, he says, come upon you, which was spoken for the others, that “I work a work which ye shall in no wise believe, though one declare it unto you.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Marvel not that it seems incredible: this very thing was foretold from the first—(that it would not be believed).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Behold, ye despisers,” as regards those who disbelieve in the Resurrection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This too might with reason be said to us: “Behold ye despisers.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Church indeed is in very evil case, although ye think her affairs to be in peace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the mischief of it is, that while we labor under so many evils, we do not even know that we have any.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What sayest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are in possession of our Churches, our Church property, and all the rest, the services are held, the congregation comes to Church every day.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but one is not to judge of the state of a Church from these things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From what then?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whether there be piety, whether we return home with profit each day, whether reaping some fruit, be it much or little, whether we do it not merely of routine and for the formal acquittance of a duty (ἀφοσιούμενοι).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who has become a better man by attending (daily) service for a whole month?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is the point: otherwise the very thing which seems to bespeak a flourishing condition (of the Church,) does in fact bespeak an ill condition, when all this is done, and nothing comes of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would to God (that were all), that nothing comes of it: but indeed, as things are, it turns out even for the worse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What fruit do ye get from your services?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Surely if you were getting any profit by them, ye ought to have been long leading the life of true wisdom (τἥς φιλοσοφίας), with so many Prophets twice in every week discoursing to you, so many Apostles, and Evangelists, all setting forth the doctrines of salvation, and placing before you with much exactness that which can form the character aright.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The soldier by going to his drill, becomes more perfect in his tactics: the wrestler by frequenting the gymnastic ground becomes more skilful in wrestling: the physician by attending on his teacher becomes more accurate, and knows more, and learns more: and thou—what hast thou gained?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I speak not to those who have been members of the Church only a year, but to those who from their earliest age have been attending the services.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think you that to be religious is to be constant in Church-going (παραβάλλειν τᾕ συνάξει)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is nothing, unless we reap some fruit for ourselves: if (from the gathering together in Church) we do not gather (συνάγωμεν) something for ourselves, it were better to remain at home.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For our forefathers built the Churches for us, not just to bring us together from our private houses and show us one to another: since this could have been done also in a market-place, and in baths, and in a public procession:—but to bring together learners and teachers, and make the one better by means of the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With us it has all become mere customary routine, and formal discharge of a duty: a thing we are used to; that is all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Easter comes, and then great the stir, great the hubbub, and crowding of—I had rather not call them human beings, for their behavior is not commonly human.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Easter goes, the tumult abates, but then the quiet which succeeds is again fruitless of good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Vigils, and holy hymn-singing.”—And what is got by these?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, it is all the worse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many do so merely out of vanity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think how sick at heart it must make me, to see it all like (so much water) poured into a cask with holes in it!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But ye will assuredly say to me, We know the Scriptures.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If ye exemplify the Scriptures by your works, that is the gain, that the profit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Church is a dyer’s vat: if time after time perpetually ye go hence without receiving any dye, what is the use of coming here continually?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why, the mischief is all the greater.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who (of you) has added ought to the customary practices he received from his fathers?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For example: such an one has a custom of observing the memorial of his mother, or his wife, or his child: this he does whether he be told or whether he be not told by us, drawn to it by force of habit and conscience.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Does this displease thee, you ask?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God forbid: on the contrary, I am glad of it with all my heart: only, I would wish that he had gained some fruit also from our discoursing, and that the effect which habit has, were also the effect as regards us (your teachers)—the superinducing of another habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else why do I weary myself in vain, and talk uselessly, if ye are to remain in the same state, if the Church services work no good in you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, you will say, we pray.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what of that?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Not every one that saith unto Me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the Kingdom of heaven; but he that doeth the will of My Father which is in heaven.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many a time have I determined to hold my peace, seeing no benefit accruing to you from my words; or perhaps there does accrue some, but I, through insatiableness and strong desire, am affected in the same way as those that are mad after riches.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as they, however much they may get, think they have nothing; so I, because I ardently desire your salvation, until I see you to have made good progress, think nothing done, because of my exceeding eager desire that you should arrive at the very summit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would that this were the case, and that my eagerness were in fault, not your sloth: but I fear I conjecture but too rightly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For ye must needs be persuaded, that if any benefit had arisen in all this length of time, we ought ere now to have done speaking.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In such case, there were no need to you of words, since both in those already spoken there had been enough said for you, and you would be yourselves able to correct others.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the fact, that there is still a necessity of our discoursing to you, only shows, that matters with you are not in a state of high perfection.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then what would we have to be brought about? for one must not merely find fault.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I beseech and entreat you not to think it enough to have invaded the Church, but that ye also withdraw hence, having taken somewhat, some medicine, for the curing of your own maladies: and, if not from us, at any rate from the Scriptures, ye have the remedies suitable for each.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, is any passionate?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let him attend to the Scripture-readings, and he will of a surety find such either in history or exhortation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In exhortation, when it is said, “The sway of his fury is his destruction” (Ecclus. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22); and, “A passionate man is not seemly” (Prov. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25); and such like: and again, “A man full of words shall not prosper” (Ps. cxl.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11); and Christ again, “He that is angry with his brother without a cause” (Matt. v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 22); and again the Prophet, “Be ye angry, and sin not” (Ps. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 4); and, “Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in histories, as when thou hearest of Pharaoh filled with much wrath, and the Assyrian.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, is any one taken captive by love of money? let him hear, that “There is not a more wicked thing than a covetous man: for this man setteth even his own soul for sale” (Ecclus. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9); and how Christ saith, “Ye cannot serve God and mammon” (Matt. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24); and the Apostle, that “the love of money is a root of all evils” (1 Tim. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10); and the Prophet, “If riches flow in, set not your heart upon them” (Ps. lxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10); and many other like sayings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And from the histories thou hearest of Gehazi, Judas, the chief scribes, and that “gifts blind the eyes of the wise.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Exod. xxiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 8 and Deut. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Is another proud?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let him hear that “God resisteth the proud” (James iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6); and, “Pride is the beginning of sin” (Ecclus. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14) and, “Every one that hath a high heart, is impure before the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in the histories, the devil, and all the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In a word, since it is impossible to recount all, let each choose out from the Divine Scriptures the remedies for his own hurts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So wash out, if not the whole at once, a part at any rate, part today, and part tomorrow, and then the whole.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And with regard to repentance too, and confession, and almsgiving, and justice also, and temperance, and all other things, thou wilt find many examples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For all these things,” says the Apostle, “were written for our admonition.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then Scripture in all its discoursing is for our admonition, let us attend to it as we ought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why do we deceive ourselves in vain?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I fear it may be said of us also, that “our days have fallen short in vanity, and our years with haste.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Ps. lxxvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who from hearing us has given up the theatres?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who has given up his covetousness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Who has become more ready for almsgiving?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I would wish to know this, not for the sake of vainglory, but that I may be inspirited to more zeal, seeing the fruit of my labors to be clearly evident.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as things now are, how shall I put my hand to the work, when I see that for all the rain of doctrine pouring down upon you shower after shower, still our crops remain at the same measure, and the plants have waxed none the higher?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Anon the time of threshing is at hand (and) He with the fan.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I fear me, lest it be all stubble: I fear, lest we be all cast into the furnace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The summer is past, the winter is come: we sit, both young and old, taken captive by our own evil passions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell not me, I do not commit fornication: for what art thou the better, if though thou be no fornicator thou art covetous?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It matters not to the sparrow caught in the snare that he is not held tight in every part, but only by the foot: he is a lost bird for all that; in the snare he is, and it profits him not that he has his wings free, so long as his foot is held tight.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Just so, thou art caught, not by fornication, but by love of money: but caught thou art nevertheless; and the point is, not how thou art caught, but that thou art caught.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let not the young man say, I am no money-lover: well, but perchance thou art a fornicator: and then again what art thou the better?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the fact is, it is not possible for all the passions to set upon us at one and the same time of life: they are divided and marked off, and that, through the mercy of God, that they may not by assailing us all at once become insuperable, and so our wrestling with them be made more difficult.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What wretched inertness it shows, not to be able to conquer our passions even when taken one by one, but to be defeated at each several period of our life, and to take credit to ourselves for those which (let us alone) not in consequence of our own hearty endeavors, but merely because, by reason of the time of life, they are dormant?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Look at the chariot-drivers, do you not see how exceedingly careful and strict they are with themselves in their training-practice, their labors, their diet, and all the rest, that they may not be thrown down from their chariots, and dragged along (by the reins)?—See what a thing art is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Often even a strong man cannot master a single horse: but a mere boy who has learnt the art shall often take the pair in hand, and with ease lead them and drive them where he will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, in India it is said that a huge monster of an elephant shall yield to a stripling of fifteen, who manages him with the utmost ease.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To what purpose have I said all this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To show that, if by dint of study and practice we can throttle into submission (ἄγχομεν) even elephants and wild horses, much more the passions within us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence is it that throughout life we continually fail (in every encounter)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have never practised this art: never in a time of leisure when there is no contest, talked over with ourselves what shall be useful for us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are never to be seen in our place on the chariot, until the time for the contest is actually come.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hence the ridiculous figure we make there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Have I not often said, Let us practise ourselves upon those of our own family before the time of trial?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With our servants (παἵδας) at home we are often exasperated, let us there quell our anger, that in our intercourse with our friends we may come to have it easily under control.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And so, in the case of all the other passions, if we practised ourselves beforehand, we should not make a ridiculous figure in the contests themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But now we have our implements and our exercises and our trainings for other things, for arts and feats of the palæstra, but for virtue nothing of the sort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The husbandman would not venture to meddle with a vine, unless he had first been practised in the culture of it: nor the pilot to sit by the helm, unless he had first practised himself well at it: but we, in all respects unpractised, wish for the first prizes!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It were good to be silent, good to have no communication with any man in act or word, until we were able to charm (κατεπᾴδειν) the wild beast that is within us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The wild beast, I say: for indeed is it not worse than the attack of any wild beast, when wrath and lust make war upon us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Beware of invading the market-place (Μὴ ἐμβάλῃς εἰς ἀγοράν) with these beasts, until thou have got the muzzle well upon their mouths, until thou have tamed and made them tractable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Those who lead about their tame lions in the market-place, do you not see what a gain they make of it, what admiration they get, because in the irrational beast they have succeeded in producing such tameness—but, should the lion suddenly take a savage fit, how he scares all the people out of the market-place, and then both the man that leads him about is himself in danger, and if there be loss of life to others, it is his doing?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well then do thou also first tame thy lion, and so lead him about, not for the purpose of receiving money, but that thou mayest acquire a gain, to which there is none equal.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there is nothing equal to gentleness, which both to those that possess it, and to those who are its objects, is exceeding useful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This then let us follow after, that having kept in the way of virtue, and with all diligence finished our course therein, we may be enabled to attain unto the good things eternal, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
